0: Comparison based on environmental effects of nitrogen management techniques in a manure digestate case study
Nicola Paccanelli, Aronne Teli, Davide Scaglione, Gabriele Insabato & Alessandro Casula

<title>Comparison based on environmental effects of nitrogen management techniques in a manure digestate case study </title>

Nicola Paccanelli ** Aronne Teli, Davide Scaglione , Gabriele Insabatob and Alessandro Casulab "DICA Environmental Section, Politecnico di Milano, Milano Piazza L. da Vinci 32, Milan 20133, Italy; Cremona Campus, Politecnico di Milano, Cremona via Sesto 39/41, 26100, Italy (Received 3 February 2015; accepted 24 May 2015)

Due to climate issues and favourable energy market, biogas is spreading as a manure management technique. Digestate is rich in nutrient and has to be handled in order to respect the 'nitrate directive' that limits nitrogen field application in areas defined as vulnerable. In this study, we compared different nitrogen management scenarios: a non-treatment option, a biological short-cut nitrification, a complete autotrophic process (anammox) and ammonia stripping from membrane fil- tration concentrate. The environmental effect comparison was obtained with 'Cross media effects analysis' and life cycle assessment (LCA). The results were different in some aspects, especially the impacts on eutrophication. According to cross media, the best process is DENO 2, while LCA shows similar impacts for all techniques and the best solution would be the no-treatment option. The main reason to adopt a digestate treatment technique is the lack of area for a correct disposal. If LCA eutrophication results are multiplied with the hectares necessary for each technology, a result similar to that of cross media is obtained.
Keywords: digestate; N management; impacts; cross media; LCA

2: Nitrogen is one of the fundamental elements of life, as we know it. Its importance is shown by the fact that more of 70% of the atmosphere is composed of molecular nitro- gen. As a component of composed molecules, it is the fourth most abundant element in living cells.[1] As the better-known carbon, also nitrogen has its natural cycle; therefore, its distribution in different forms has been reg- ulated by natural chemical and biological processes. With the industrial revolution and the progress of chemistry, syn- thetic fertilizers were introduced in the natural cycle as a new source of nitrogen (e.g. the Birkeland method). On the one hand, their increased use in agriculture allowed reduc- ing the threat of famine [2-4] but, on the other hand, due to the fact that the biological removal processes remained the same, concentration of nitrogen (mostly in the form of nitrite and nitrate) dramatically increased in the soil and in the waters.[5] The surplus of nutrients causes eutrophica- tion problems mainly in lakes and closed seas; therefore, to deal with such issues, the European Council developed the directive 91/676/EEC, which is also known as the 'nitrate directive'. According to this regulation, areas vulnerable to nitrite were identified and the maximum manure applica- tion rate in those areas was limited to 170 kgN/ha. Such prescriptions have a heavy impact on agriculture and live- stock industry due to the increase in livestock production

[6] and the related amount of nitrogen-rich manure to be disposed. The use of such manure in biogas production has increased in recent years [7] with national incentives for green energy production and the European targets on renewable energy production [8] in relation to the climate change issue. The digestion not only allows biogas produc- tion but also affects the distribution of the different nitrogen forms; it increases the presence of ammonia and reduces the organic nitrogen.[9,10

There are different solutions for a conform digestate disposal in order to respect the limitation of N application rate imposed by the 'nitrate directive'; those imply differ- ent environmental impacts related, for example, to energy and reagent consumption as well as gaseous emissions. The goal of this study is to evaluate the environmental impacts of different processes for digestate management and treatment. As shown in Figure 1, the main purpose of nitrogen removal technique is to reduce the nitrogen applied to fields mainly because the nutrient that has to be disposed would be more (due to intensive livestock farm- ing) than the amount an available surface can receive (170 kgN/ha).

In this study, four different solutions were considered. The basic consists in the land application of the diges- tate without treatment except a raw solid-liquid separa- tion. Based on the results obtained by the BRAIN project

(a research study conducted by the 'Politecnico di Milano' and the University of Florence to assess the applicabil- ity of innovative biological processes of nitrogen removal as well as to support the economic and environmental sustainability of biogas production), two biological tech- niques were analysed. The first is based on the nitritation and denitrification without the formation of NO3 as an intermediate (short-cut nitrification), which is called later as DENO 2. The other is based on complete autotrophic process (partial nitritation + anammox), which is there- fore called later as ANAMMOX. Also a physical-chemical method was considered [11]; it consists in ammonia stripping from a concentrate obtained with membrane filtration.

Several researchers have studied the impact of bio- gas and of nutrient removal technologies, but they either focus on civil wastewater treatment plants, for example, Rodriguez-Garcia et al. [12], or they do not consider nutri- ent removal, for example, Rehl and Müller [13]. The present study is, to the best of our knowledge, the first one to focus on the impacts of agricultural biogas diges- tate nutrient management. The comparison techniques that were used are the 'Cross media effects analysis', devel- oped by the European Commission, and the life cycle assessment (LCA).

7: An overview of the analysed system is presented in Figure 1. As a first step, there is a solid-liquid separation of the digestate. This could be done with a low-efficiency, screw press separator, or, if the liquid fraction is treated to remove

nitrogen with a high-efficiency, centrifuge separator.[14 After separation, the solid fraction and the liquid fraction are stocked from 8 to 180 days and emit CH4 N2O and NH3. The liquid fraction is treated with DENO2, ANAM- MOX or membrane concentration and ammonia stripping to remove nitrogen (in the form of ammonia). During the treatment, gases are released, and reagents and energy are consumed. After treatment, the liquid fraction is stocked, but the low concentration of nutrients, which could gen- erate emission, made us avoid considering the impacts of such phase. Since the treated liquid fraction has a low concentration of organic matter (in fact, acetate has to be added as COD source to allow the nitro-denitro process) and biomass is almost absent because it is separated via sedimentation, we considered that CH4 emission and N2O emission analysis could be avoided. Ammonia emissions were not considered too because the treatment purpose was to remove NH3; therefore, there is a low concentra- tion of ammonia in the liquid fraction. Since emissions are ruled by Henry's law, and ammonia is only partially present as free ammonia, we hypothesized that NH3 emis- sions (and for similar reasons, also CH4 and N20) were of the same magnitude as the uncertainty of the field appli- cation emission factors. Therefore, we did not consider emission in the after-treatment stocking phase of the liquid fraction. In order to consider also the impacts due to field application and to evaluate the efficiency of each technol- ogy in a surface-limited scenario, the hectare necessary to spread the digestate were calculated. The surface needed was obtained considering the total nitrogen disposed, in both liquid and solid fractions, after treatment to be 170 kgN/ha, according to the directive (Figure 2).

Considering the impacts of transport, we supposed a distance between the field and the plant of 1 km, the field application 200 m and that 20 tons per time were trans- ported. The gases released by the digestate during field application, as well as leaching of nitrite, were also con- sidered. For every case, the emission factor for field appli- cation is hypothesized to be the same and obtained from literature.[15-20] It was also supposed (as for the stocking after treatment of the liquid fraction) that, due to the fact that the ready biodegradable organic substrate is used in anaerobic digestion, and a residual CH4 emission is con- sidered in the stocking after solid-liquid separation, there are no methane emission in field application of both solid and liquid fractions. Figure 3 shows the nitrogen flow in the considered processes.

Considering the impacts of transport, we supposed a distance between the field and the plant of 1 km, the field application 200 m and that 20 tons per time were trans- ported. The gases released by the digestate during field application, as well as leaching of nitrite, were also con- sidered. For every case, the emission factor for field appli- cation is hypothesized to be the same and obtained from literature.[15-20] It was also supposed (as for the stocking after treatment of the liquid fraction) that, due to the fact that the ready biodegradable organic substrate is used in anaerobic digestion, and a residual CH4 emission is con- sidered in the stocking after solid-liquid separation, there are no methane emission in field application of both solid and liquid fractions. Figure 3 shows the nitrogen flow in the considered processes.

The basic decision, in choosing a treatment method, is whether those methods show a better environmental performance than do no treatment at all. 11: DENO2, a biological technique used to remove ammonia from the liquid fraction of biogas digestate, was widely analysed during the BRAIN Project and the data used were obtained from that pilot plant. It uses nitrifica- tion (in aerobic conditions) and denitrification (in anoxic conditions) processes performed by autotrophic biomass. Both these processes produce nitrite as intermediate prod- uct. To reduce the treatment cost, this technique avoids the formation of nitrate intermediate and directly yields gaseous molecular nitrogen from nitrite. The first step, the nitrification of ammonia to nitrite, is caused by bacteria

called AOB (ammonia oxidizing bacteria); these bacteria also cause the denitrification of nitrite to molecular nitro- gen. The second step, the nitrification of nitrite to nitrate, is carried out by bacteria called NOB (nitrite oxidizing bacte- ria); these also cause the denitrification of nitrate to nitrite. In typical operative conditions (temperature around 20°C, neutral pH and no stress of the bacteria due to low oxy- gen) in nitrification, the slower kinetic is the one performed by the AOB; there is no accumulation of nitrite because the removal rate by NOB is faster than its formation rate by AOB.[21] In order to reduce the operative cost, par- tial nitrification has been considered; it allows avoiding the

formation of nitrate by operating in conditions in which NOB are inhibited. Usually, this is achieved by limiting oxygenation (AOB are more competitive than NOB on oxygen) or by specifically designing the hydraulic sys- tem (AOB and NOB have different growth rates; SO it is possible to wash out the NOB and maintain AOB in the reactor).[22]

By analysing the different stoichiometric coefficients, it appears that partial nitrification has a 25% lower oxygen request and needs 40% lesser carbon source compared with complete nitrification and denitrification, thus being more cost-efficient.[23]

14: The anammox bacteria Brocadia anammoxidans were dis- covered in 1995 in Holland [24], but better identification and characterization were only presented in 1999.[25] A first approximation of the overall process of partial nitritation and anammox [26] was expressed with the formula (1).

Compared to the conventional nitrification and denitri- fication processes, anammox allows half oxygen demand and there is no need of external carbon. The completely autotrophic process (partial nitritation and anammox) is cost-effective, thanks to lower aeration and external carbon source requirements; also, the sludge treatment and dis- posal are significantly reduced.[27] A critical issue could be the emission of N2O that was found to be related to high nitrite concentrations.[28] The anammox process was studied and applied mainly for the treatment of the liq- uid fraction of municipal sludge digestate of waste streams from the food industry (such as digested potato wastew- later, and fish canning effluents) of leachate, and recent study demonstrates the applicability also for wastewater in the mainstream.[29, In recent years, there has been much interest in the application of the process for manure digestate.[31]
15: <header>Case 3 - membrane concentration combined with ammonia stripping </header>

This technology uses physico-chemical laws to remove nitrogen.[ After high-efficiency solid-liquid separation, indispensable for any treatment technology, as well as the stocking, the liquid fraction is ultrafiltrated with plane membranes with share filtration. The SO obtained solids are treated as those obtained from solid-liquid separa- tion. In order to have an additional volume reduction, the liquid fraction is concentrated with reverse osmosis pro- cess. The concentrate is charged with quicklime (CaO) to increase pH and move the dissociation equilibrium of ammonia towards free ammonia instead of the ionic form.

The solution is then inflated and, thanks to Henry's law, the ammonia is transferred from liquid solution to gas solution. The ammonia-enriched gas flows then through washing towers with a sulphuric-acid-rich solution. The low pH and the sulphuric acid allow the formation of ammo- nium sulphate crystals that precipitate at the bottom of the tower. Such particulates can be used as synthetic fertilizer or in chemical industries. The low remaining ammonium content in the liquid fraction is applied to the field.
16: For a full-scale treatment plant, the environmental impact analysis should consider construction, operation and dis- mantling. Due to the small and complex correlation between the materials used at pilot plant and at full-scale plant, as well due to lack of data regarding the construction of these, the impacts of construction and dismantling were not taken in to account. Therefore, only the operation stage was analysed, including background processes associated with reagent and energy provision, and the disposal of the digestate.
17: In such case, it is typical to have a low-performance solid-liquid separation (a screw press was considered) that allows an easier field application of the solid and liquid part. Such solid-liquid separation (as given in Table 1) does not need to add polyelectrolyte and has only energy consumption obtained from literature.[10] The amount of nitrogen, as well its repartition in organic and ammonia, in the liquid and the solid after separation, was obtained from literature. [32]

The stocking, of both solid and liquid fraction, is con- sidered to last 180 days before field application. Methane emissions are supposed to happen while stocking instead of after field application. Italian law prescribes the amount of nitrogen present in the solid and liquid fraction after stocking [33]; the same amounts were used in this analysis.
The Case 0 has no treatment; SO there are no emissions due to the treatment.

25: As "functional unit (or base unit)," for the compari- son 1000 kg of nitrogen, as Total Kiljedan Nitrogen, in the input digestate was considered. This allows an eas- ier comparison between the technologies and is more intuitive. Two comparison methods are presented. Cross media effects analysis allows a simpler evaluation of the impacts and is used in the European Union to com- pare similar technologies and determine the best. LCA allows to consider avoided emission by avoided produc- tion of products and has a deeper analysis which, however, reduces the transparency of the calculation; LCA has com- monly been used to study similar problems in wastewater treatment. [35-40]

Cross media, due to its relative simple analysis, does not consider the benefits of avoided production of ammo- nium sulphate, which is generated as a by-product in Case 3. In order to deepen the analysis, and highlight the difference, in LCA Case 3, a sub-case was introduced, in which the benefits of the production of ammonium sulphate (and therefore it is avoided impacts) are taken into account.
26: This method was developed to determine the Best Avail- able Techniques (BAT reference document on Economics and Cross Media Effects 2006) under Directive 96/61/EC concerning integrated pollution prevention and control. BAT takes into account the likely cost and benefits of measures and aims to protect the environment by con- sidering the whole process and its impacts. To assess the environmental effects for each of the alternative techniques under consideration, different pollutants are identified in an inventory and are collated into seven environmental impact categories based on the environmental effects that the pol- lutants are most likely to cause. This allows the comparison of different pollutants. The categories are:

<list>Human toxicity expressed as kg lead equivalents 
global warming expressed as kg CO2 equivalent 
aquatic toxicity is the quantity of water (m³) required to achieve the predicted no effect concentration in water 
acidification expressed as kg SO2 equivalent 
eutrophication expressed as kg phosphate ion equiv- alent 
photochemical ozone creation potential expressed as kg ethylene equivalents.</list>

These categories give a comprehensive coverage of the most relevant environmental effects while still ensuring that the assessment remains practical and relevant. The evaluation approach used consists of a simple compari- son between the results from each of the environmental category.

28: Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a technique to assess envi- ronmental impacts associated with all the stages of a prod- uct's life or a production technique.[41] LCA evaluates the environmental concerns by:

<list>Compiling an inventory of relevant energy and mate- rial inputs and environmental releases; 
Evaluating the potential impacts associated with identified inputs and releases; 
Interpreting the results to help make a more informed decision.</list>

To obtain a detailed analysis of the impacts and the characterization of the impacts of each treatment phase, the SimaPro programme and its Ecoinvent library were used. Only midpoint results were analysed; ReCiPe mid- point with European normalized parameters was used, in order to obtain a more detailed comparison of each technique/case and an easier interpretation of the results. Therefore, no weight was assigned to each impact. Of the impacts presented by the method, only those of interest were considered:

<list>climate change (CO2 equivalent); 
human toxicity (1.4-diclorobenzene equivalent); 
particulate formation (PM10 equivalent); 
terrestrial acidification (SO2 equivalent); 
marine eutrophication (N equivalent), later called just 'eutrophication'.</list>

The impact called 'Fresh water eutrophication' was not considered because it refers to phosphor and the study analysed nitrogen effects. LCA considers as benefits the impact of the missing production of a good. Case 3 (membrane concentration combined with ammonia stripping) allows us to obtain ammonium sulphate, which can be used as a primary source in other applications. In the LCA, an additional case was analysed; in order to obtain a raw sensibility anal- ysis of Case 3, also a case without ammonium sulphate recovery was considered.

Table 2. Results of the cross media analysis in percentage variation with respect to the no-treatment option.

|Case 0 No treatment|Case 1 DENO2(%)|Case 2 ANAMMOX(%)|Case 3 Membrane + stripping(%) Human toxicity|Base 0|2.8|2.9|- 2.4 Global warming|Base 0|5.3|28.7|- 48.3 Aquatic toxicity|Base 0|61.6|60.4|- 52.8 Acidification|Base 0|2.8|2.9|- 2.4 Eutrophication|Base 0|61.6|60.4|52.8 Photochemical ozone creation potential|Base 0|- 3.2|34.9|60.7

Case 0 (no treatment). The human toxicity index is reduced in a similar way in all treatments and the best performance is achieved by the anammox technology. In the analysed cases, cross media considers only ammonia emissions as source of human toxicity. Those emissions present a cumu- lated value similar in all cases and therefore the impact shows little difference between each case.

The impact on global warming increases with the DENO 2 technology mainly due to the N2O emissions, which could be reduced with a better control in the dosage of COD. The most reduction is obtained with the membrane concentration and ammonia stripping technol- ogy; although it presents the highest energy consumption (almost double the energy needed by anammox treatment), it has the lowest N2O emissions, which have a high global warming potential (GWP100 = 298). Aquatic toxicity is drastically reduced with all technologies, especially with the biological ones. In the considered system, aquatic toxi- city depends on NO2 pollution expressed as leaching. This has the maximum values in Case 0 and the lowest in the biological treatments.
The acidification is influenced by the same sources, ammonia emissions, as human toxicity and therefore has the same reduction percentage.

One of the main goals of the EU Directive 91/676/EEC is to reduce and avoid eutrophication and thus it is logical to notice a marked reduction in the impacts of the nitrogen removal technologies on eutrophication. The best values are obtained with biological methods and in particular with DENO 2.

The photochemical ozone creation potential reflects the cumulated nitrous oxide emission in each case. CH4 emis- sions have a very small conversion factor and are lower than N2O; therefore, the photochemical ozone creation potential follows the distribution of nitrous oxide between
each case. Thus, the best results are obtained by Case 3 followed by Case 2.

Most relevant changes are present in three impacts: global warming, aquatic toxicity and eutrophication. In two of these (aquatic toxicity and eutrophication), the best results are achieved with the biological technique DENO 2, but this has the worst results in the global warming impact.

33: <header>LCA results </header>

This analysis allows us to take into account several factors that are not in cross media; it consents a deeper analy- sis but, on the other hand, makes its comprehension less clear. First, a comparison of the aggregate results was con- ducted; a more detailed study on the biological treatments (Case 1 and Case 2) was carried out because those were the only technologies where emissions while treatment were considered.
In Table 3 and Figure 4 are reported the results of the overall comparison obtained with LCA analysis.

The worst case for climate change is Case 1; the best one is Case 2, which has even lower impacts than the no-treatment option. The good performance of anammox is mainly due to it lower methane and nitrous oxide emissions compared to Case 0, although it has higher energy con- sumption (from treatment and high-efficiency solid-liquid separation) and consumes polyelectrolytes. Compared to the other treatments, Case 2 has the same methane emis- sions and polyelectrolytes consumption, but it has the low- est energy consumption and does not need any additional consumable substance.

Human toxicity has the lowest impact values in Case 0 and the highest in Case 1. Considering only the treat- ments, the lowest impact is obtained with Case 3 treatment only if the ammonium sulphate is reused; if not, the best

treatment is anammox. This shows the impact of the reuse of by-products that cross media does not consider. It also highlights that in LCA, more parameters influence human toxicity and not mainly ammonia emission (which is sim- ilar in all cases). The results obtained by Case 2 can be explained by the side effects of acetate consumption because it is the main variable that distinguishes it from the other nitrogen removal technologies.

In particulate formation and terrestrial acidification, all solutions have very similar impacts; the one with lesser than the others is anammox. Those impacts are mainly ruled by ammonia emissions [42] and therefore anammox, with the less emissions, has the best performance, while in Case 3, the higher energy consumption leads to the worst performance.

The eutrophication has very interesting results. All the technologies for nitrogen removal have similar impacts and the one with a little better performance is the anammox technique. It is surprising to notice that the no-treatment option has the best results in all categories except terrestrial acidification. This could be explained by considering the fact that also in the no-treatment option, the respect of the EU Directive 91/676/EEC was hypothesized and this case has the lowest energy consumption and does not need any

reagent. The main problem associated with such directive is that usually, there is not enough land for manure disposal and therefore the 'no treatment' is not an option and there has to be a nitrogen removal technique in order to reduce the nitrogen load to the amount allowed by the directive. If the impacts on eutrophication are normalized with the limiting factor, hectares necessary to dispose the residual nitrogen, a different picture is obtained. Considering the product of the impact on eutrophication and the hectares that are necessary for a correct disposal of the remain- ing nitrogen content (in percentage referred to the worst case), the best results are obtained by DENO 2 (45%), fol- lowed by Anammox (46%) and membrane concentration and ammonia stripping (71%), and the worst case is the no-treatment option.

Solid- liquid separation has a high impact in both treatments, but as it is the same in every treatment, it appears that the high value of human toxicity of DENO 2 is (compared to the other technologies) due to a mix of higher emission and the consumption of acetate (the energy consumption appears to have a low impact during the treatment phase because the highest consumption is in Case 3, but it has much lower impact on human toxicity than DENO 2). 40: <header>LCA results </header>

This analysis allows us to take into account several factors that are not in cross media; it consents a deeper analy- sis but, on the other hand, makes its comprehension less clear. First, a comparison of the aggregate results was con- ducted; a more detailed study on the biological treatments (Case 1 and Case 2) was carried out because those were the only technologies where emissions while treatment were considered.
In Table 3 and Figure 4 are reported the results of the overall comparison obtained with LCA analysis.

The worst case for climate change is Case 1; the best one is Case 2, which has even lower impacts than the no-treatment option. The good performance of anammox is mainly due to it lower methane and nitrous oxide emissions compared to Case 0, although it has higher energy con- sumption (from treatment and high-efficiency solid-liquid separation) and consumes polyelectrolytes. Compared to the other treatments, Case 2 has the same methane emis- sions and polyelectrolytes consumption, but it has the low- est energy consumption and does not need any additional consumable substance.

Human toxicity has the lowest impact values in Case 0 and the highest in Case 1. Considering only the treat- ments, the lowest impact is obtained with Case 3 treatment only if the ammonium sulphate is reused; if not, the best

It also highlights that in LCA, more parameters influence human toxicity and not mainly ammonia emission (which is sim- ilar in all cases). The results obtained by Case 2 can be explained by the side effects of acetate consumption because it is the main variable that distinguishes it from the other nitrogen removal technologies.

In particulate formation and terrestrial acidification, all solutions have very similar impacts; the one with lesser than the others is anammox. Those impacts are mainly ruled by ammonia emissions [42] and therefore anammox, with the less emissions, has the best performance, while in Case 3, the higher energy consumption leads to the worst performance.

The eutrophication has very interesting results. All the technologies for nitrogen removal have similar impacts and the one with a little better performance is the anammox technique. It is surprising to notice that the no-treatment option has the best results in all categories except terrestrial acidification. This could be explained by considering the fact that also in the no-treatment option, the respect of the EU Directive 91/676/EEC was hypothesized and this case has the lowest energy consumption and does not need any

reagent. The main problem associated with such directive is that usually, there is not enough land for manure disposal and therefore the 'no treatment' is not an option and there has to be a nitrogen removal technique in order to reduce the nitrogen load to the amount allowed by the directive. If the impacts on eutrophication are normalized with the limiting factor, hectares necessary to dispose the residual nitrogen, a different picture is obtained. Considering the product of the impact on eutrophication and the hectares that are necessary for a correct disposal of the remain- ing nitrogen content (in percentage referred to the worst case), the best results are obtained by DENO 2 (45%), fol- lowed by Anammox (46%) and membrane concentration and ammonia stripping (71%), and the worst case is the no-treatment option.

41: The impacts of three different treatment options were anal- ysed with two different methods. According to the results obtained, there is no technique that has better results in all impact categories. The cross media comparison allows a better under- standing of the evaluation process (thanks to its sim- plicity); thus, LCA allows a more detailed analysis and considers also the impacts of reagent consumption and the avoided impacts by the reuse of a by-product. The results of the evaluation method are therefore different in some aspects, especially the impacts on eutrophication differed. With cross media, the best process against eutrophication is DENO 2, while with LCA, the impacts of the technolo- gies are very similar and the best solution would be the no-treatment option. The main reason to adopt a digestate treatment technique is that there are not enough hectares for its correct disposal. If the impacts on eutrophication obtained with LCA are multiplied with the hectares nec- essary for each technology, a result similar to that of cross media is obtained.
Results of LCA show that Case 3 becomes a feasible option only if the ammonium sulphate is reused; if not, anammox should be preferred.
The main environmental issues in biological techniques are the N2O emissions, which should be taken care with an accurate plant management.

In order to reduce the impacts (mainly in particulate formation, terrestrial acidification and marine eutrophica- tion), the stocking of the solid part should avoid, or at last reduce, ammonia emissions.
